entry.title: How to derive the master data values from existing transactional data using data action?
entry.content:
Hi, 

Here is the scenario,

There are four dimension as Factory, Location, Type, Account in the model.

 

FactoryLocationTypeAccountValue
ASeattle#Sales Qty150
ASeattleXPrice10
BCalifornia#Sales Qty95
BCaliforniaYPrice7

I want to write a data action logic which checks if factory is "A" and location is "Seattle" then it should derive the values as "X" type for Sales Qty account based on transactional data dimension combination of Price row and similarly if factory is "B" and location is "California" then it should derive the values as "Y" type for Sales Qty account based on transactional data dimension combination of Price row.

In short, I want to derive the values of master data based on existing transactional data in the model using data actions.

Anyone who has faced similar kind of issue or knows the workaround in this scenario. Their comments would be deeply appreciated.

Thanks.

entry.title: Manage Risk-free Rate (RFR) Interest Rate Instrument with Accumulating Condition
entry.content:
Foreword

With the progress of IBOR (Inter-bank offered rate) transition, more and more financial instruments (such as OTC interesting bearing instruments, bonds, interest rate swaps, etc.) are adopting alternative nearly risk-free rates (RFRs), like SOFR for USD, €STR(ESTER) for EUR, SONIA for GBP, SARON for CHF, etc.

This article outlines the process of creating a typical SOFR-based Interest Rate Instrument transaction using product type 55A which is pre-delivered in best practices configuration content of SAP in S/4HANA Public Cloud, Treasury and Risk Management Solution, and this transaction utilizes the accumulating condition function which is available since 2308 release.

Step-by-Step Guide

1. Create the 55A investment transaction.

On the Structure tab, enter the necessary parameters.

Go to the details of the Normal Interest condition and change parameters:

Go to the Dates tab of Nominal Interest condition, basically the ‘dates’ information here reflects that interests are paid weekly, and the first due and payment date is 02.05.2024.

Go to the Interest Rate Adjustment condition, and change the parameters.

(Note: all of the above maintained data in the transaction should be based on the contract details specified by counterparty.)

Go to Conditions Overview, select the Nominal Interest condition, and then choose Create Accumulating Condition.

On the dialog box, choose condition type 1205 (which is from SAP Best Practices Configuration Content for Public Cloud since 2308 release), and choose OK.

Confirm the Eff. From date (= eff. from date of nominal interest condition) by choosing Copy.

On the next screen, you will see the details for the new accumulating condition within a new condition group 7500. You may check the ‘dates’ information of this accumulating condition, which should be carried from and equal to the ‘dates’ information of the Nominal Interest condition.

Go to the Cash Flow tab, by default the accumulating interest flows (1205) are displayed.

By clicking button Show/Hide Single Flows, the single interest flows (1200) will also show up.

With this kind of setup in the transaction, we have achieved the objectives:

  • The single interest flows (1200) are calculated based on daily SOFR rates and compounded daily (except weekend on which the Friday rate is used, and Friday ~ Sunday are calculated and compounded together), within one interest period.
  • The last single interest flow of an interest period uses the SOFR rate of previous day (because of lockout period = 1 working day).
  • An accumulating interest flow (1205) sums up the single interest flows of this interest period.
  • Only accumulating interest flow will be relevant to posting and payment, which will be explained later.

Here, from the screen-shot, you see all single flows are using percentage rate 5.322 (=5.31+spead 0.012), that’s because so far the daily SOFR rates imported into system are up to 24.04.2024, therefore SOFR rate 5.31 on 04.24.2024 is used for calculation of future single interest cash flows in this transaction for the time being.

Save the transaction.

2. Settle the transaction (screen-shot is not provided here).

3. Run TBB1 for the initial principal increase flow on 25.04.2024 (screen-shot is not provided here).

4. Now, assume the month end (30.04.2024) is reached.

The SOFR rates until 30.04.2024 have been imported into system.

After SOFR rates are imported, you can update the cash flows via scheduling job for TMFM or changing the transaction (screen-shots are not provided here)

Check the cash flows after being updated:

The last single interest flow of the 1st interest period (cal. from 01.05.2024 to 02.05.2024) uses the SOFR rate on 30.04.2024, because the lockout period = 1 working day specified in Interest Rate Adjustment condition in this example.

5. Although the interest rate fixings have not been conducted for the single interest flows up to 30.04.2024 (via TJ05/TI10), we can run TPM44 to accrue the interest income up to 30.04.2024 based on the forecasted cash flows.

Run TPM44 on key date 30.04.2024 (and key date is included) for one valuation area:

The 4 single interest flows up to 30.04.2024 are selected (although they are displayed by using the accumulating interest update type MM1205+ in the log), and accumulated amount (889.21 USD) is used for accrual posting (instead of accrual posting per each single interest flow).

Before running TPM44, the configuration in IMG activity Assign Update Types for Accrual/Deferral (configuration activity ID 102925 in public cloud) should be completed, for the accumulating interest flow’s update date type (MM1205+), although it is accumulated on the basis of single interest flows (MM1200+), and a Grouping Term should be specified here.

6. Now, the time has come to 02.05.2024, on which the interest payment for the 1st interest period should happen.

And at this point in time, the SOFR date of 01.05.2024 has been known and entered in system.

Run TJ05 to fix interest rates up to 01.05.2024:

Afterwards, check the cash flows in deal:

In this example, the last single interest flow of the 1st interest period (cal. from 01.05.2024 to 02.05.2024) still uses the SOFR rate on 30.04.2024, even if rate of 01.05.2024 is already imported, because the lockout period = 1 working day specified in Interest Rate Adjustment condition.

Nevertheless, the newly imported SOFR rate of 01.05.2024 is used to update the forecasted single interest flows of next interest period. 

Now, the relevant single interest flows in the 1st interest period has the Interest Rate Adjustment Status ‘Fixed’, and relevant single interest flows and accumulating interest flow have posting information ‘Flagged for Fixing’, which means TBB1 can be executed to fix and post the interest payment for the 1st interest period.

7. Run TBB1 up to and including due date 02.05.2024 (display one valuation area as example): posting is only carried out for accumulating interest update type MM1205+. Posting is NOT carried out for single interest update type MM1200+, that’s because when a single interest condition is accumulated by accumulating condition, the relevant single interest flows will become irrelevant to posting, although the posting relevant configuration is complete for single interest update type.

If payment request should be generated for interest flow, then it is also only relevant to the accumulating interest flow.

After running TBB1, check cash flows in transaction again: now single interest flows have posting information ‘Fixed (not relevant for posting)’, and accumulating interest flow has posting information ‘Posting carried out’.

If checking in TPM13, you can’t see the single interest flows, because they are not transferred from transaction management to position management.

Appendix:

More information regarding IBOR transition could be found from SAP notes:

  • 2939657 - Composite SAP Note: EU Benchmark Regulation, Risk-Free Rates (RFRs) - shared basis for CML and TRM
  • 2932789 - Composite SAP Note: EU Benchmark Regulation, Risk-Free Rates (RFRs), TRM
  • 3328022 - Accumulating Interest Flows: Collective Note

entry.title: Difference b/w these subquery and inner join? I am getting different results.
entry.content:
Hello!
I get correct amount of entries with below query:

    lt =   SELECT tab1.act_id
                    FROM table1 AS tab1
                    INNER JOIN :it_score AS score
                    ON score.id = tab1.id
                    WHERE tab1.bb IN (
                                      -- BBIDs
                                      SELECT tab2.BB_ID
                                      FROM table2 AS tab2
                                      INNER JOIN :it_score AS score
                                      ON score.id = tab2.id
                                      WHERE tab2.si = :iv_si
                                         ) ;

But more with below query, which is not as expected but I want to go for a join instead of subquery.
I understand this could be because of how data is in tables as well, but I also want to know why.
I have failed after multiple tries.

lt =  SELECT tab1.act_id
      FROM table1 AS tab1
      INNER JOIN :it_score AS score
        ON score.id = tab1.id
      INNER JOIN table2 AS tab2
        ON tab1.bb = tab2.BB_ID           
      AND score.id = tab2.id              
      WHERE tab2.si = :iv_si;

this is also wrong result, idk why:

lt =  SELECT tab1.act_id
      FROM table1 AS tab1
      INNER JOIN :it_score AS score
        ON score.id = tab1.id
      INNER JOIN table2 AS tab2
        ON tab1.bb = tab2.BB_ID     
      INNER JOIN :it_score AS score2     
      ON score2.id = tab2.id           
      WHERE tab2.si = :iv_si;

some help would be appreciated, how do I get same result as my subquery?

Thanks

entry.title: Disable EK02 SD Condition - calculate cost - performance issue & impacts
entry.content:
I am experiencing performance issues with sales order creation in SAP via integration. I have identified that the issue occurs due to cost calculation, as outlined in note https://me.sap.com/notes/2569300/E. I have reviewed the configurations (controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Control of Sales-Order-Related Production/Product Cost by Sales Order).

The note states: “In the customizing of the requirement class, please check field 'Costing ID'. The field should have value A (Automatic costing) or B (Automatic costing and marking). This means that the sales order item will be costed (marked) automatically during saving. Resolution: You should check if you really need the sales order cost estimate. If not, then you can change the requirement class, so that the system doesn't automatically calculate each item. If you decide that you need the cost estimates, you can still consider to not do that automatically during saving of the sales order but asynchronously via T-code CK55.”

The client indicated that they do not use this condition when saving the order but find it useful for subsequent price determination studies. Therefore, I removed the value “A” from the configuration and left it empty, and the condition was no longer calculated, as expected. I executed CK55 after creating the order, and the condition was calculated correctly. This resolves the issue as the interface will function, and the calculation will be done after the order creation process.

Now, I need your help to understand if there are any other impacts with this configuration in CO, cost, or anything else. I would like to confirm whether this configuration only disables this calculation without affecting other processes.

entry.title: Maximum quantity limit for line items in sales orders
entry.content:
Hi SAP expert ,

Business scenario: The customer's main business is selling building materials and accessories, and its ultimate customer is villa owners. In a sales order, it is necessary to record the detailed list of building materials and accessories for each villa in the sales order. A villa has approximately 3000 building materials and accessories, and there will be a demand for 10 villas in one sales order. Therefore, one sales order will have 30000 lines of project details.

I tried to use the Sales and Distribution Bom feature, the header of the bom is each villa . the items of the bom is the detailed list of building materials and accessories , but I found that the sales orders still display the detailed materials of the BOM, so that the number of line items of each sales order will be greater than 20000 .

I need to confirm the maximum number of line items for a sales order in the system, and how many detailed line items can the system support for a single sales order ?

entry.title: SAP GR & IR migration from Legacy ERP
entry.content:
SAP Goods Receipt & Invoice Receipt history migration

 

 

  1. Overview:

Purchase Order is one of the most critical objects which serves as backbone of any procurement process for an organization. It is also one of the legal binding documents for both buyer and seller. A purchase order is a legal document form used by a buyer and sent to a supplier for an order. A purchase order specifies items, quantities, prices, and credit terms for a purchase from the vendor. A PO becomes a legally binding contract when a vendor accepts the purchase order.

 

There are multiple transactions posted to a purchase order but primarily 2 key documents posted are:

  1. Goods Receipt (GR)
  2. Invoice Receipt (Goods & Freight)

 

Any point of time we migrate from one ERP to another, we do see multiple open transactions on Purchase orders, few briefed below:

  1. PO fully GRed not invoiced
  2. PO Partly Gred not invoiced
  3. PO not GRed but Partly invoiced
  4. PO not GRed but fully Invoiced
  5. PO GRed greater than PO Quantity, Invoice posted equals or less than GR
  6. PO not GRed but invoiced greater than PO

 

It is practically not possible to close all Purchase Orders during cutover in Legacy system and convert fully open orders. It is equally not possible to repost all GR or IR again in SAP after migration as it will impact cutoff GL balances and inventory count/value.

 

  1. Problem Definition:

 

During course of ERP migration to another ERP, treatment of open Purchase Order transactions is one of the biggest challenges and undergoes multiple discussions how to treat them. Such transactions can’t be left as is due to the reason that they are core business transactions and needs to be stay in ERP (New) for day-to-day operations. In this document we will tak about migration from Legacy ERP to SAP migration.

 

Below are few key reasons why we need to bring in Legacy GR & IR during migration process:

  1. Legacy Partial GR – to post remaining GR to match PO quantity
  2. Legacy Partial GR – to post vendor invoice for payment
  3. Legacy GR – Return of goods Receipt in new ERP (SAP)
  4. Subsequent reversal of vendor invoice
  5. Modification in existing Purchase Order with transactions
  6. Unmatched GR & IR - Account Assignment (MR11) in SAP
  7. Posting Goods in Transit to Goods Receipt
  8. Closing GL balance match with SAP GRIR Clearing Account

 

All these listed business scenarios are common and are necessary to be catered to avoid any business disruption post migration to SAP.

 

  1. Solution in SAP:

 

Standard SAP doesn’t provide any straight standard solution to address this need. Most of the customers either close purchase orders in legacy ERPs and migration only fully open POs or they do not migrate POs at all and post subsequent transaction on open Legacy POs manually using JV or other material management manual transactions.

 

However, SAP did provide a solution in past with old ECC 4.5B era, and it was not removed in subsequent versions. The solution could be used in current versions and is capable to addressing most of the requirements. As this is old program and SAP has stopped support, we need to make certain code modifications with the help of technical ABAP team to meet our business requirements. I personally designed and used it for one of the Fortune 500 company with most complex business scenarios and huge volume of open GRIR transactions during migration and it worked perfectly fine for all 80+ countries.

 

Note: solution highlighted in this document posts financial postings and updates MM table EKBE but doesn’t not impact inventory quantity and doesn’t update GR or IR MM tables like RBKP, RSEG, MSEG e.t.c. Migration of GR & IR history doesn’t consider quantity or price variance and will post exactly what the values are passed. However, SAP will consider the values migrated for subsequent calculation of quantity or price variance while posting normal PO transactions.

 

Let me now take you thought the technical solution for migration of GR & IR history from Legacy ERP to SAP.

 

There are 2 key standard SAP programs which are required to design a solution listed below. we need to create a wrapper program to call below 2 programs to mass upload whole GRIR migration using upload functionality. This will require technical development

  1. RM06EEEX (Tcode OMQ4) – to create a file with all data mapping and values
  2. RM06EEI1 ( Tcode OMQ2) – to upload and post file created by OMQ2

 

 

Preparatory Step: set up logical file path t.code FILE: (to be defined each client)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Above values comes by default from SAP, however, physical path (AL11) needs to be defined which is used to place and read file internally by program.

 

RM06EEEX (Tcode OMQ4):

All the key fields are to be mapped which needs update to converted purchase order. These values are required to be passed in Transfer Structure: Purchase Order Header (MBEPOH ) and Transfer Structure: Purchase Order Item (MBEPOI). Below are the required fields to be passed in Transfer Structure:

 

 

 

 

 

Transfer Structure: Purchase Order Header (MBEPOH)

  1. Purchasing Document (EBELN)

 

Transfer Structure: Purchase Order Item (MBEPOI)

  1. EBELP – PO Item number
  2. WEREF - GR/IR History indicator (1=GR, 2=IR and 3=GR & IR)
  3. WEMNG – Legacy GR Quantity
  4. WEBTH – Legacy GR Amount in PO currency
  5. WEMBP – Legacy IR quantity
  6. REBTH – Legacy IR amount in Local currency
  7. REBTF - Legacy IR amount in document currency
  8. WAERS – Legacy IR document currency

 

RM06EEI1 ( Tcode OMQ2):

 

Second step is to define physical file path which is used to read and convert data. This also includes actual financial and material management postings.

 

 

GL Accounts: we define GL accounts which will get posted during GRIR migration program. Flow of postings will be as below.

 

GR History Migration:

Dr. Contra Account GR

Cr. GRIR Clearing Account

 

IR History Migration:

Dr. GRIR Clearing Account

Cr. Contra Account GR

 

Document Type:

Define document type to be used by program to post Goods receipt and invoice receipt.

 

GRIR Clearing account will post exactly like normal Purchase Order postings and hence can be used for subsequent GR & IR clearing.

 

The postings are purely GL to GL posting with no impact on inventory quantity and visible in table EKBE. GRIR migration postings will be visible in PO history tab as below. Migration will create a document with line item number always as 9999.

 

 

 

Post GRIR migration, important step is to initiate production startup for material ledger using t.code CKMSTART:

 

 

Input all plants and enter POH in command field and hit enter key:

 

 

 

Below fields will get enabled for input:

 

 

Select PO History only and execute.

 

This will activate all POs where history is migrated and are good to be used for subsequent transactions.

 

If above step is missed, user will get below error while posting GR or IR using normal transactions:

 

 

  1. Key Points:

 

  1. PO history once migrated can’t be reversed. Only financial postings can be reversed using t. code FB08 but will not remove it from PO history tab
  2. PO history can be migrated to PO only once. Thus, if there are multiple line items in PO, migrate history for all line items in one go
  3. This solution doesn’t have option to migrate freight clearing history and hence freight component needs to be added to PO value during PO conversion
  4. GR history migrated, if needs to be reversed, user need to post GR reversal using movement type 102 using standard GR reversal transactions and hence impact inventory
  5. IR history migrated, if needs to be reversed, user need to use standard IR reversal transactions MR8M and hence impact vendor sub ledger
  6. Any error in GR or IR migration will warrant manual adjustments or scrapping and recreation of PO and uploading GR/IR migration.
  7. Standard SAP Account Assignment MR11 works post migration of legacy GR/IR values

entry.title: Re: SAP Asset Manager - delta sync ignoring syclo changes sent by transport
entry.content:
Hi Radek,

Yes, is the correct behaviour. In delta sync, a calculation of data (object IDs) based on relevant changes is done first. Then this data is populated based on the filters configuration in the oMDO. However, if there is no relevant data, further processing is cancelled (it has no sense to apply filters to no data…).

In other words, changes in the filter configuration, do not trigger nor affect the delta sync mechanism.

So to see the effect of the changes you have to do what you noticed: make changes in some relevant data so the next delta sync retrieves this data, or reset the app to trigger an initial sync.

Best regards, Gonzalo.

entry.title: Re: Migration of EHANA to Datasphere by leveraging Native HANA Cloud Instance of Datasphere Tenant
entry.content:
@shabana  Thank you for your detailed response. I appreciate the clarification, especially regarding the limitations around advanced analytics capabilities within the HANA Cloud tenant of SAP Datasphere.

However, after reviewing few more SAP documents, I noticed a few points that seem slightly different from your recommendations. In the training content from the "SAP Datasphere Fundamentals" (reference images attached from the training deck), it was highlighted that SAP Datasphere supports advanced analytics capabilities via the Open SQL schema. The slides outlined features such as:

- "APL" and "PAL" support in the Open SQL schema.
- Native integration of Python and R for data science and machine learning, leveraging "HANA Cloud" functionality (spatial, text, and graph functions).
- The ability to create views, tables, execute stored procedures, and write data directly into the Open SQL schema.

Given that these features were presented as part of the product's capabilities, it’s a bit confusing to reconcile that with the limitations you mentioned regarding multi-model and ML use cases like leveraging APL and PAL. Also, referring to the blog (link) from one of the SAP Product experts.

Could you please help clarify below? 
- Is the use of Open SQL schema limited to "PoC" purposes and not intended for full production environments?
- could you please elaborate on the specific concerns SAP foresees when migrating HANA calculation views to the HANA Cloud instance of DataSphere? 

Understanding this distinction would greatly help align the migration strategy with SAP’s intended best practices and avoid any future roadblocks.

Thanks again for your insights, and I look forward to your clarification.

samarjit.saha@sap.com, Would appreciate your perspective from a migration standpoint. While we understand that the migration path may not be straightforward, migrating HANA calculation views to the HANA Cloud instance of DataSphere doesn’t fall under advanced analytics use cases. Could you add your perspective and advise regarding this migration?

entry.title: Re: events in abap
entry.content:
EVENTS in SAP ABAP

 

SAP ABAP is one of the Event triggering programming languages which has its unique way of executing the code by calling the events which user has mentioned at the time of implementing the code. SAP has prioritized the order of execution in ABAP, Lets see what are all those events.

I have seen people saying there are 8 events, and some say there are 10, some other say there are only 6 events……but to my knowledge I say there are exactly 12 events in SAP ABAP.

(1) Load of Program — This is the first event which gets triggered once the execution is started. Goal of this event is to load the code into system memory.

(2) Initialization — This event is used to assign the default values to the variables which we define in the data decleration section.

(3) At Selection Screen Output — This event is mainly used to perform screen manipulations in the output selection screen. We can refer this event to a module pool concept called PBO ( Process Before Output ) which works in the similar way. Inoder to deal with At selection screen output we have to use table called Screen. This event will ge triggered before the selection screen is processed.

(4) At Selection Screen — This event is mainly used to perform validations on multiple input field parameters. This event will get triggered while the selection screen is being processed.

At Selection Screen On ‘Field’ — This event is used to perfomr screen validatons on single input paramter field.

At Selection Screen On-Value Request For ‘Field’ — This event is used to enable an F4 functionality on input field.

At Selection Screen On-Help Request For ‘Field’ — This evenet is used to generate a documentation relevant to that field upon clicking F1.

(5) Start-Of-Selection — This is the place where our whole business logic is implemented. This event gets triggered after the selection screen processing is completed.

(6) End-Of-Selection — This represents end of start of selection event. This event is mainly used while we are dealing with Logical Database which we often used in HR Module. Its not a madatory event to mention when we are not dealing with Logical database, despite we have to use it when dealing with logical database.

(7) Top-Of-Page — This event is mainly used if you want to mention header on the output screen. We often use this event while dealing with Reports.

(8) End-Of-Page — This event is mainly used if you want to mention the footer on the output screen.We often use this event while dealing with Reports. Unlike in Top of Page, we have to reserve some space to mention footer part on the output screen.

Syntax → REPORT ZAging_Report LINE-COUNT (20).

(9) At Line-Selection — This event gets triggered when the user double click on List line in the final output screen.

(10) Top-Of-Page During Line-Selection — This event is used to write something on top of every page of secondary list.

(11) At PF — This event gets triggered when the user clicks on Function Button.

(12) At User-Command — This event gets triggered when the user clicks on any Custom button of the GUI.

Above are the list of events in SAP ABAP. If I was wrong at any point ,leave your suggestions in the comment window.

as far as I know, you just need the Cloud Application Studio (Pilot) IDE to implement AddOns in your ByD Tenant: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CLOUD_APPLICATIONS_STUDIO/cbcebe3cfb1647a8b0322c18dbb0b481/751bc8d273e21014bfa4f7ac03265b0e.html?locale=en-US

Regards

entry.title: Functional creating CAP application for recording and tracking eating sensations using SAP Joule
entry.content:
Introduction

In today’s fast-paced world, understanding our eating habits and sensations can provide valuable insights into our overall well-being and improve our performance in work. Inspired by concept from “The Personal MBA by Josh Kaufman” about how tracking your food sensations could improve a little bit your performance in work and eager to test AI capabilities from SAP Joule, this blog post will guide you through the process of creating a CAP (Cloud Application Programming) application designed to record and track eating sensations.

The main goal of this proof of concept is to demonstrate that functional users, even those without extensive coding backgrounds, can effectively generate and comprehend code using powerful AI tools like SAP Joule and Microsoft Copilot Webbrowser. By leveraging these advanced technologies, we aim to bridge the gap between functional expertise and technical implementation, empowering more individuals to contribute to innovative solutions.

Join me as we explore the steps to build this application, from initial setup to deployment, and discover how AI can enhance our ability to create meaningful and user-friendly applications.

Creating and translating requeriment using Microsoft Copilot

Creating a requirement in your native language and then translating it can be much easier than doing it directly in English. In this case, I decided to add to the prompt to simplify and make the requirement much more understandable for later programming. The quality of the documentation and its understanding is essential for artificial intelligence to program correctly.

 Once we have the requirement, we will set up an SAP BTP account to test creating a CAP application using SAP Build and SAP Joule without extensive knowledge of these technologies.

IMPORTANT: In this test, we will only program using natural language.

Set-up SAP Build Code using boosters

First of all, we need to get the SAP Build application running on our SAP BTP account. In this case, I don’t have technical knowledge in SAP BTP, but we can use boosters to have a fully operational SAP Build programming environment without needing extensive knowledge.

Setup SAP Build Code in SAP BTP | SAP Tutorials

Special thanks to Paola Laufer for the tutorial.

Programming application using SAP Joule and understanding details with Microsoft Copilot

IMPORTANT: Highlight that the steps used were taken from material provided by SAP and adapted to my application. Once again, special thanks to Paola Laufer for this SAP Build Code Test Drive that has served as my guide.

Create a Full-Stack Application with SAP Fiori UI using Joule in SAP Build Code | SAP Tutorials

Let’s get started with our application!

Artificial intelligence always needs to be reviewed and adapted by humans. Although this could probably have been done through a series of prompts, I have adapted the requirement to a format that I believed SAP Build would understand better.

 Create Data Entities with SAP Joule

Using the requirement and the SAP Joule option in SAP Build Code, we can specify that we want to create a CAP application and generate the necessary data models and services.

1. Prompt SAP Joule with requirement:


2. Accept the response:


3. Results:


Enhance the Application Data Using SAP Joule

The data for testing applications must be real and reflect reality. Generating these datasets can be a tedious task that consumes a lot of time and imagination. You’ll never have to do this again because SAP Joule can generate the data you need to work on your application!

1. Prompt to generate data:

2. Accept and trigger the information for your testing table:

3. AI makes mistakes, but we review and correct them by filling in some fields that were left empty:

 

Sorry my mistake here, I should request to SAP Joule to delete 'minutes' column because it's duplicated with 'hour'.

Create Application Logic with SAP Joule

Now it’s time to add logic to our application to ensure that the behavior of the fields is as expected.

1. Add logic:


2. Prompt SAP Joule with data validation logic that you want to perform:

 3. SAP Joule triggers a proposal of the code:

4. As I'm a noob in CAP language, I've requested to Microsoft Copilot to ensure that all steps are good and to understand the code:

5. Once code is reviewed and understood that works fine, this code is accepted and trigger to the application:

Add UI to the Application

We have all the code now; it’s time to create the screens to enter and view the information. Let’s do this!

 

1. Create UI application:

2. Most important step here is to decide which actions will be performed by this application. In this case, we've selected 'Form Entry Object Page' because UI application will be responsible to collect data from user:

3. UI application is created with few easy steps:

4. Repeat steps for application which is responsible to show information. In this case, we need to select 'List Report Page':

5. Finally, we can show how both applications are created:

Run application

Everything is ready! Now let’s run the application and test it.

Overview about 'Food tracking for your job performance' application

Application screen

The requirement called for two applications: one to add food items and another to review the data. We have successfully met this requirement:

Add a new entry

The requirement called for help fields to enter the data and only allow certain values.

The day and time fields have the correct drop-down menus.

But it wasn’t able to create a drop-down menu for the ‘Type of food’ and ‘Transmitted Sensation’ fields (it probably could have done this by iterating the system). Despite not having a selection menu for ‘Type of food’ and ‘Transmitted Sensation’, the system was able to create validations to ensure the correct field is selected.

 

Show your data

The system was able to generate the table to display and it works correctly, showing the entry added in the previous step:

 

Additionally, the requirement asked to group by ‘Day’, ‘Type of food’, and ‘Transmitted Sensation’, and this requirement has been met:

 

Conclusion

The application requirement wasn’t complicated, but I believe it’s enough to demonstrate that for simple application cases, we can use SAP Joule to generate a fully operational application. As we can see from the analysis, the application meets 90% of the requirements and only failed in generating the drop-down menu.

For me, the conclusion is clear: without any CAP knowledge, I was able to create an application in less than 45 minutes and generate a blog in less than 2 hours. Now, I’m excited to see what I could achieve with a bit more time!

End credits

Time is very valuable and creating blog easy to understand and with high quality content are difficult with few time. AI can help us doing it and you have an example with this post which mainly created using Microsoft Copilot (AI).

 Time consumed for this PoC: 2.5 hours of a boring Sunday

entry.title: Re: Global Trade Management - From Business Suite to S/4HANA
entry.content:
Dear @flots ,

thank you very mich for you interest in GTM. Unfortunately there are no Best Practices Processes delivered by SAP. This is why you will not find GTM in Signavio or SAP Cloud ALM. The only documentation you find in help.sap.com.

In SAP-Note 1539636 - GT: Basic Implementation Guide for GTM - SAP for Me you find an outdated implementation guide for SAP ECC.

For S/4HANA my team at ADventas and I have developed such best practice processes. Please write me a pm if you want to get to know them.


Best
Peter

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP Error during OData GET request "504 Gateway T...
entry.content:
Hi,

I had similar case where I had to increase the timeout of the service in CAP. As a solution I added the following two parameters in the mta.yaml file for the approuter module:

- INCOMING_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT

- timeout on the required destination

The timeout on the destination solved it but I also kept the other property:

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: How can we apply filters on navigation properties of...
entry.content:
Not entirely sure but I think it would something like this:

SELECT.from(EntityA,a => {
            a.field1,
            a.field2,
            a.field3,
            a.toEntityB(b=>{
                b.fieldB1,
                b.fieldB2
            }),
            a.toEntityC(c=>{
                c.fieldC1,
                c.fieldC2
            })
        }).where({ fieldB1:'ValueX' }))

You can also try to write it in sql and parse it:

 const query = cds.parse.cql ( `SELECT a.field1, a.field2, a.field3, a.toEntityB.fieldB1, a.toEntityB.fieldB2, a.toEntityC.fieldC1, a.toEntityC.fieldC2 FROM EntityA as a where a.toEntityC.fieldC2 = '${value}'`)

You can also create a view as described here:

https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/cds/cdl#views-with-inferred-signatures

entry.title: Synchronizing Attributes from Azure to IAS
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm encountering an issue with synchronizing certain attributes such as country, department, city, manager etc., from Azure to IAS.

I adding these parameter in IPS  as follows:

aad.user.attributes=id,mail,userPrincipalName,employeeId,displayName,givenName,surname,jobTitle,city,companyName,country,businessPhones,mobilePhone,department

Many Thanks you for any tips.

Best Regards

 

 

 

 

 

{
    "user": {
        "condition": "$.userPrincipalName EMPTY false",
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$",
                "targetPath": "$"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mailNickname",
                "targetPath": "$.externalId",
                "optional": true,
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.schemas[0]",
                "constant": "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mail",
                "targetPath": "$.emails[0].value",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userPrincipalName",
                "targetPath": "$.userName",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$.displayName",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.givenName",
                "targetPath": "$.name.givenName",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.surname",
                "targetPath": "$.name.familyName",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mobilePhone",
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[0].value",
                "optional": true,
                "defaultValue": "888-888-888"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[0].type",
                "condition": "$.mobilePhone EMPTY false",
                "constant": "mobile"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.businessPhones[0]",
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[1].value",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[1].type",
                "condition": "$.businessPhones.length() > 0",
                "constant": "work"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.groups",
                "targetPath": "$.groups",
                "optional": true,
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.manager.id",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['manager']['value']",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.city",
                "targetPath": "$.addresses[0].locality",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.addresses[0].country",
                "targetPath": "$.country",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.employeeId",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['employeeNumber']",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['resourceExtensions']['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['department']",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['department']",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.manager.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['manager']['displayName']",
                "optional": true
            }
        ]
    },
    "group": {
        "ignore": false,
        "mappings": [
            {
                "targetPath": "$.schemas[0]",
                "constant": "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:Group"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mailNickname",
                "targetPath": "$.externalId",
                "optional": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$.displayName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.members",
                "targetPath": "$.members",
                "optional": true,
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.members[*].id",
                "constant": "value",
                "optional": true,
                "type": "rename"
            }
        ]
    }
}

entry.title: Re: Task List with Material VS equipment BOM
entry.content:
Hi, 4HI is Proactive Maintenance, so yes you are correct if you want to, you could just create a General Task List, then add that to the Maintenance Order and that will add all the materials to it in the components tab.

Re. Equipment BOMs and Task Lists, they are different objects with different business use cases.

General Task Lists are typically made with reference to a particular maintenance activity, inspection, repair job (Operations, Documents, Materials etc). And then can be used across many Maintenance Orders if required.

Equipment BOMs can be used to build out the structure of your equipment a bit more and link the spare parts (materials) that are specific to that piece of equipment. This is not shared across other equipment when its setup, its just so that a Maintenance Planner can see the spare parts when they are scoping out a Maintenance Order.

Here are some links in case you want to read up in more detail:

  • Maintenance Task Lists 
  • Maintenance Bills of Material 

Tim

entry.title: Re: Issue with Dynamic Table Access for Copying MOLGA Content Between Tables
entry.content:
V_T52D1 is a maintenance view, not a database table.

Whenever you are doing dynamic code try and run the code that is generated in the program inserted into a test program as-is (not dynamically) to see if it works. By this I mean get the dynamic code from the debugger when you run the program to test it, and then test that code in a temp/test program to see if it compiles and also to see if it works correctly.

I added this to a test program:

"SELECT * FROM v_t52d1 INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_temp) . "

And the syntax check gave me this error:

This makes sense:

I would propose you select from these tables directly using the join condition supplied in the maintenance view within the DDIC.

entry.title: Re: SAC Calculate after aggregation based on datasphere
entry.content:
Use SUM exception aggregation type for GP%, and specify all of the available dimensions as exception aggregation dimensions.

If that does not work, Use SUM exception aggregation type for both GP & GP%, and specify all of the available dimensions as exception aggregation dimensions.

SAP has specifically mentioned about the behavior here 

Aggregations in Formulas | SAP Help Portal

Also check this

Price * Volume Formulas | SAP Help Portal

LMK

-Savio

entry.title: Re: SAC User Stats - Need a report which provides informa...
entry.content:
Go to the SAC Administration panel: Navigate to Main Menu → System → Monitor → Auditing.
Filter Audit Logs: Use filters to search for specific events such as user logins, changes to stories, models, etc.
You can filter by time range, event type (login, model updates, story updates), and the specific user.

To get the stats on Changes to models, stories, and other content , check Content Lifecycle Management (LCM) logs:

Main Menu → System → Content Network → Manage → Content Updates.

You can also check Monitoring tab in SAC

Main Menu → System → Monitor → Users or Sessions.

The Users tab will show active users, their roles, and last login times. This can be helpful for tracking who is accessing the system. The Sessions tab provides details on user sessions (time logged in, IP addresses, etc.).

entry.title: Re: SAC Application designer story blended models/lined d...
entry.content:
• Did you Ensure the blended models are correctly linked and configured. Ensure that all dimensions and measures used in the table are correctly bound and mapped to their respective models. If any are missing or unbound, SAC may throw this error.
  • If you've added any custom scripts for clicks or interactions, ensure they correctly reference datasets from the different models.
  • Look for where the getdatasetid is used, and check if it’s properly retrieving the ID. If the model or dataset is not loaded or is null at the time of the click, that would trigger the error.

entry.title: Re: Currency conversion is not functioning incorrect setu...
entry.content:
Dear OwenLiu,

Thank you for your response,

We are posting Post General Journal entries for normal JV transactions (SA) in EUR, considering that our company code currency is HKD, while our client requires the reporting in USD.

As per SAP’s suggestion, we have set USD as the functional currency and have maintained the currency exchange rates for EUR to USD and USD to EUR. However, it seems that the system is converting EUR to HKD first, and then HKD to USD. This has resulted in discrepancies between the reports generated by the client and our SAP reports.

My exchange rates which we have maintained for the January Month.

Specifically, we are posting an amount of EUR 15,420, which should convert into USD 16,808.19 at an exchange rate of 1.09.

However, it appears that the system is improperly converting EUR to HKD at an exchange rate of 8.53, resulting in a value of HKD 131,535.67. Following this, the conversion from HKD to USD is occurring at an exchange rate of 7.81, which leads to a total of USD 16,841.96.

This sequence of conversions deviates from the expected outcome, and as a result, we are seeing a discrepancy of USD 33.77.

The expectation is that the system should convert directly from EUR to USD. Ideally, our company code currency should be USD; however, as per SAP public cloud standards, the country currency by default is set as the company code currency. The users require only the transaction currency to convert to the functional currency, which is intended to be USD for accurate reporting.

Please let me know how we can address this issue moving forward or if there's any additional information you need from my end.

SELECT

entry.title: Mass close production orders
entry.content:
How to change production order status to closed automatically once the production order status changes to Finished. 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Mass close production orders
entry.content:
When a production order is released, a production lot is created. The planned structure (operations, activities, and reporting points) and the planned data of the production order are copied to the production lot and used as a basis for creating production tasks and default values. 

When the production tasks and, consequently, the production lot are finished, the production order is automatically finished. 

When the production lot is closed, the production order is automatically closed as well. 

Depend on above, you can create a Lot Closure Run to close the mass production lot automatically and ,consequently, the production orders will automatically closed.

Please refer to the documentation for more information. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_BYDESIGN/2754875d2d2a403f95e58a41a9c7d6de/2ddeccfe722d101494c086745aa81807.html?version=2202#create-a-lot-closure-run

 

Create a Lot Closure Run steps:

  1. Choose the Lot Closure Run subview.

  2. Click New to open the New Automated Lot Closure Run quick activity.

  3. On the General Data view, enter an ID and a description for the run.

  4. Specify further selection criteria for the run using the tabs available. The system automatically includes or excludes production orders based on these criteria. In the lower table, on each tab page, click Add Row to add a new row.

    • Filter production lots using a selection range

      In the Inclusion/Exclusion field, click the arrow and choose Inclusion or Exclusion. In the Search Pattern field, click the arrow and choose Equals. In the From and To fields, select the start and end range for selection.

    • Filter production lots with reference to a particular selection

      In the Inclusion/Exclusion field, click the arrow and choose Inclusion or Exclusion. In the Search Pattern field, click the arrow and choose the appropriate operator, for example Less than or Equal To. In the From field, select the business document for the selection, for example Product ID 8186.

    Repeat the steps outlined above to specify the selection criteria for each relevant tab.

  5. At the top of the New Automated Lot Closure Run quick activity, click Save. Then click Close to return to the Automated Actions view.

    Note

    You can choose to activate the run from here, or you can activate the run later, just before scheduling. Click Set to Active to activate the run from here. To activate the run later, select the run you want to activate. Click Actions and then choose Set to Active.

    The new lot closure run has been created and saved to the system. You can now define a schedule for this run.
